title: Brandy snaps with whipped cream
subtitle: Lace biscuits rolled hot, filled with cream
excerpt: Brandy snaps in the British tea-table tradition: a thin lace biscuit of butter, sugar, golden syrup and flour, baked flat then rolled around a wooden spoon while still hot. Filled with whipped cream and served at tea. Twelve filled snaps.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the brandy-snap recipe in the sweet-biscuits chapter (note: the brandy is optional in many household recipes from the period; ginger is the constant). Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: documents the lace-biscuit form and the rolling technique. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents the cream filling and the autumn-fair association.

recipe.freezeNotes: The unfilled snaps keep crisp in an airtight tin for 3 days; do not freeze (the cream filling weeps).
recipe.batchNotes: Do not double — the rolling window is narrow, and a second tray's worth of biscuits sets too quickly to roll.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the snaps in the morning, fill within an hour of serving (cream softens the biscuit on contact).

recipeTools.notes: Two trays so one can rest while the other is rolling.
The handle is the rolling former.

glossaryTerms.definition: Brandy snaps come out of the oven soft and pliable; within about a minute on the tray they cool enough to roll cleanly around the handle of a wooden spoon; after another minute they harden and crack rather than bend. The rolling window is two minutes wide. Work fast and in small batches (four per tray maximum); if a snap sets before you can roll it, return the tray to the oven for 30 seconds to soften.

content.text: Brandy snaps are a lace biscuit: butter, sugar, golden syrup and flour cooked together to a smooth molten batter, dropped in teaspoons onto a baking tray, baked until the surface looks lacy and golden, and rolled while still hot around the handle of a wooden spoon. The handles cool inside the snaps and slide out; the cooled biscuit is hollow, crisp, and ready to fill with whipped cream.
Bake in small batches. The rolling window is narrow: about two minutes wide, and a tray of twelve snaps will harden before you can roll them all. Four snaps per tray is the working number.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan and line two baking trays with mats or well-greased baking paper. Grease the handle of a wooden spoon. Put the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{caster-sugar}} and {{golden-syrup}} into a small saucepan over a low heat and stir until smooth; the mixture should be melted and slightly thinned but not boiling. Take off the heat, add the sifted {{plain-flour}}, the {{ginger-ground}}, the zest of one {{lemon}} and the {{brandy}} if using. Stir to a smooth thin batter.

Drop teaspoons of batter onto one tray, four to a tray, with 10 cm between each — they spread to about 9 cm. Bake for 7–8 minutes; the snaps will spread and turn deep gold with a lacy surface. While they bake, set up the second tray ready to go in.

Take the tray from the oven and put the second one in. Wait one minute for the snaps to firm enough to lift. Lift each with a palette knife, drape across the greased wooden-spoon handle, and roll quickly into a tube; slide off and set on a rack to cool. Work fast — the
rolling window is two minutes wide
. If a snap hardens before you can roll it, return the tray to the oven for 30 seconds to soften.

Whip the {{double-cream}}, {{icing-sugar}} and {{vanilla-extract}} to soft peaks. Spoon into a piping bag and pipe into both ends of each cooled snap. Serve within an hour; the cream softens the biscuit on standing.
